attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old woman presents with a history of three first-trimester miscarriages. She has regular menstrual cycles and no significant medical history. Transvaginal ultrasound reveals a single uterine fundus with normal external contour, but the endometrial cavity appears to be divided by a midline tissue band extending from the fundus.

During embryonic development, the female reproductive tract forms from the Müllerian (paramesonephric) ducts through a series of carefully regulated steps including fusion and canalisation.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following best describes the embryological process that leads to the clinical findings in this case ?
options: Incomplete lateral fusion of Müllerian ducts with normal septal resorption
Complete fusion with abnormal endometrial differentiation
Defective canalisation after normal septal resorption
Incomplete resorption of the midline septum after fusion
Excessive apoptosis of fused Müllerian tissue
attributes.explanation:
A septate uterus results from incomplete resorption of the midline septum after fusion of the Müllerian ducts. This creates a fibrous or muscular septum that partially or completely divides the uterine cavity, while maintaining a normal external uterine contour.

The incorrect options can be explained:

  • Incomplete lateral fusion of Müllerian ducts with normal septal resorption would result in a bicornuate uterus with an abnormal external contour
  • Complete fusion with abnormal endometrial differentiation would affect the entire endometrium rather than creating a septum
  • Defective canalisation after normal septal resorption would result in absence or stenosis of the uterine cavity
  • Excessive apoptosis of fused Müllerian tissue would create defects in the uterine wall rather than a septum

Understanding these embryological processes is crucial as different uterine malformations have different clinical implications and management approaches. A septate uterus is associated with increased risk of miscarriage and can be treated with hysteroscopic resection of the septum.
learningPoints: The uterus forms from fusion of the Müllerian (paramesonephric) ducts during weeks 8-12 of embryonic development.
Failure of Müllerian duct fusion or canalisation leads to various uterine abnormalities including septate, bicornuate, and unicornuate uteri.
A septate uterus results from incomplete resorption of the midline septum after fusion of the Müllerian ducts.
A septate uterus typically presents with recurrent pregnancy loss or infertility.
references: Jayaprakasan K, Ojha K. Diagnosis of Congenital Uterine Abnormalities: Practical Considerations. J Clin Med. 2022 Feb 25;11(5):1251. doi: 10.3390/jcm11051251. PMID: 35268343; PMCID: PMC8911320.

Akhtar MA, Saravelos SH, Li TC, Jayaprakasan K; on behalf of the Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists. Reproductive implications and management of congenital uterine anomalies. Scientific Impact Paper No. 62. BJOG. 2019 Nov 20; DOI: 10.1111/1471-0528.15968.
